learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate mastery of key theories and practices in your chosen genre/format while engaging respectfully with diverse narrative perspectives, including Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander perspectives, across writing, publishing, and production contexts.
Develop advanced research and project management skills.
Provide contextualised, constructive feedback to industry standard.
Revise, edit and proofread to industry standard.
Identify key principles and emerging trends in the publishing and film production industries.

assessments.category: Creative Production/ Exhibition, Paper/ Report/ Annotation
assessments.task_description:
A project proposal for your advanced writing project. Using the template provided this should include:

  • Working title (e.g., 'A Heartbreaking Work of Staggering Genius')
  • Description of genre and sub-genre (e.g., 'Fiction; dark fantasy')
  • Project summary
  • Personal Creative Statement
  • Research and self-directed reading plan
  • Writing, revision and editing schedule
  • Write the proposal to 800 words, then
  • Provide approximately 200 words of drafted creative writing to support the proposal


The proposal should be 800 words and the creative writing sample approximately 200 words.

If you use AI or MT in completing this assessment, you must declare this by including the WRIT3000 Assignment Cover Sheet – AI Usage with your submission, available on the course Blackboard site.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit alongside your AI statement as detailed on Blackboard in the Assignment folder.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Task description — Writing Exercise Assessment, based on lecture material. Completed in the lecture, in 20 minutes.

From Week 2 to Week 11 there will be a written exercise held in the lecture (8 exercises in total). During the lecture you will be given 20 minutes to handwrite 150 to 200 words of creative writing responding to a specific concept raised in that week's lecture material.

There are 8 opportunities across the semester, each worth 6%. Only your best 5 results count, together forming 30% of your final grade. Because only 5 of the 8 count, the assessment already builds in the flexibility to miss up to 3 sessions — for illness or any other reason — with no penalty and no documentation required.

There are no opportunities to re-take an individual exercise, and no alternative assessment is available except in the specific circumstances set out below.

As this is an internal course, you must make yourself available to complete each exercise at the scheduled date, time and venue.

These are handwritten, closed-book tasks: no written material, electronic material or electronic devices may be used. All such items must remain in your bag or under your desk, and all electronic devices must be switched off or in airplane mode. If a device emits any noise, you may be subject to an allegation of misconduct and the item may be confiscated for the duration of the exercise. You may bring suitable writing implements (in a clear container), water in a clear bottle with the label removed, and disposable earplugs.

The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools is not permitted for this task.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit written paper to course staff in the lecture, immediately after each exam is finished.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date: Participation will be assessed wholistically across ten tutorials
assessments.task_description:
All students are expected to participate actively in course activities. Participation will be assessed wholistically through regular in-class writing tasks which can be further developed at home then brought to class for further workshopping and development.

Participation is marked out of 10 at the end of semester against Marking Criteria published on Blackboard, informed by the tutor's weekly record across the ten graded tutorials. Marks reflect involvement in the learning of the group: participation, preparation of each week's workshop task, and the quality of feedback given to peers. Attendance alone does not earn marks. The mark reflects demonstrated engagement with the workshop process.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Participate in seminars and tutorials by following in-class writing and workshopping tasks, which will be reviewed in-situ by your course tutors.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Within your chosen genre, write either:

  • one complete, self-contained short text; or
  • the opening of, or an excerpt from, a longer-form text.


For a prose-based work of fiction or non-fiction, this should be 2,200–2,500 words.

For a screenplay, this should be 12–15 pages, properly formatted (equivalent to 2,200–2,500 words).

Each work must include a title and a short, one- or two-sentence logline (included in the word count).

If you use AI or MT in completing this assessment, you must declare each instance by including the WRIT3000 Assignment Cover Sheet – AI Usage with your submission, available on the course Blackboard site.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit via Turnitin and include your AI statement as detailed in Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4 Lecture - What Research Can’t Give You

Guest writer Dr Martine Kropkowski (Everywhere We Look, Ultimo Press 2024; Left Behind, 2025) examines the limits of the archive and the place of lived and cultural knowledge in the creation of Fiction, CNF, and film. Her work bridges empirical inquiry and felt experience, with essays in Griffith Review, Overland and The Conversation.

The set text for this lecture is her book, Everywhere We Look, available via the course Talis link.

Writing Exercise 2

Learning outcomes: L01, L02

learning_activities.topic:
Week 6 Lecture - Structure as Meaning

Structure read as an authorial decision rather than a template: the shape of a story as part of what it means. Guest writer Jack Dann (internationally award-winning author and UQ Adjunct Senior Research Fellow) takes the clearest case: alternate history, where changing a single event restructures an entire world, and the story's architecture becomes its argument. Drawing on his Fiction Writer's Guide to Alternate History (Bloomsbury Academic, 2023), with considerations across prose and screen.

Fiction Writer’s Guide to Alternate History is the set text, available via the Talis link.

Writing Exercise 4

Learning outcomes: L01, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 6 Lecture - Structure as Meaning Structure read as an authorial decision rather than a template: the shape of a story as part of what it means. learning_activities.topic:
Week 9 Lecture - The Personal in Telling True Stories

With guest writer Carly-Jay (CJ) Metcalfe, author of Breath (UQP 2024, winner of the 2024 Queensland Literary Awards People’s Choice Book of the Year) and UQ MPhil candidate. CJ will discuss memoir, the self on the page, and the ethics of writing real lives: her own and those of others.

Breath is the set text, available via the Talis link.

Writing Exercise 7

Learning outcomes: L01, L05
